course_title: Ruling the World: Crisis and Contestation in World Politics

learning_outcomes.description: Identify global crises and their forms of contestation.
Analyse the relationship between global crises and contestation.
Demonstrate an understanding of the interplay between theory and practice in world politics.
Communicate effectively and persuasively, both in writing and orally.
Develop policy responses to global crises.

assessments.task_description:
The assessment has 2 components (15 marks in total)

1) Lecture Quiz (8 marks; 1 mark per quiz)

Beginning in Week 2 and concluding in Week 12, students will complete a short written quiz during each lecture. Each quiz will consist of 1 question based on the content covered in the previous week's lecture. Students will have 10 minutes to respond. The timing of the quiz will vary from week to week and may occur at any point during the scheduled lecture, depending on the structure of that week's class.

There will be 11 quiz opportunities throughout the semester. However, students are only required to complete 8 quizzes. The top 8 quiz results will count towards the final grade. This means that students may miss up to 3 quizzes without any penalty.

There are no opportunities to re-sit individual quizzes, and no alternative assessment will be provided except in exceptional and documented circumstances approved under University policy. Students are therefore highly encouraged to complete quizzes whenever possible rather than relying on later opportunities.

As the final examination draws heavily on lecture material, consistent participation in quizzes will also support exam preparation and enhance overall course performance.

Quiz responses will be assessed on the quality and accuracy of their content rather than grammar, spelling, or writing style. However, students are expected to write legibly so that their responses can be assessed.

Alternative Assessment

If you have a Student Access Plan that applies to the quizzes, please contact your Course Coordinator as soon as possible.

Many accommodations outlined in Student Access Plans can be implemented during the lecture (for example, accommodations for students who are unable to handwrite assessments). Students will be given 10 minutes to complete one very short question. This timeframe already includes additional time to support students who may require it. If your Student Access Plan entitles you to further time beyond the allocated 10 minutes, please contact the Course Coordinator as early as possible to discuss appropriate arrangements.

If you have a documented reason for being unable to attend lectures and therefore miss more than 3 quizzes, you may be eligible for a make-up quiz later in the semester. You are not eligible for the make-up quiz if you:

  • Have made other commitments that conflict with the lecture schedule in advance.
  • Fail to plan ahead and miss multiple quizzes early in the semester.
  • Miss 1 or 2 quizzes due to short-term illness or other minor disruptions (students may miss up to 3 quizzes without any impact on their grade).

The make-up quiz is reserved for students who have a documented reason for missing more than three quizzes. Examples may include:

  • Serious illness or injury requiring an extended absence;
  • Documented circumstance resulting in a lengthy period away from classes.

To be considered for alternative assessment, you must have a Student Access Plan, contact the Course Coordinator and provide supporting documentation before the end of Week 3.

If an unforeseen change in circumstances occurs after this date (for example, illness), please contact the Course Coordinator as soon as possible and provide supporting documentation.

Further information about the quizzes and assessment criteria will be provided on Blackboard.

2) Tutorial Activities (12 marks; 1.5 marks per tutorial for 8 tutorials)

For each of the 8 tutorials, students may receive 1.5 marks. Please note that attendance is a prerequisite but does not, by itself, guarantee a mark. To receive a participation mark, students are expected to:

  • Arrive on time.
  • Listen attentively to others.
  • Demonstrate a willingness to speak and participate in class discussions and activities.
  • Contribute constructively to tutorial discussions and activities.
  • Engage respectfully with other students.

This assessment task is to be completed in person.

A detailed, measurable, and criteria-referenced marking scheme will be available on Blackboard.

Alternative Assessment

If you are unable to attend 1 tutorial due to unforeseen and exceptional circumstances, you may be eligible for up to 2 make-up opportunities during the semester. To receive a make-up mark, you must submit a review of the two essential readings for that week to your tutor by the following week.

Please note that you must obtain your tutor's approval in advance. Approval is not automatic and will be considered on a case-by-case basis. Make-up opportunities are intended for unforeseen circumstances only and are not available for ongoing work, study, or other regular commitments.

The Use of AI

The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools is not permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
This oral presentation assesses course content covered up to and including Week 5.

10 minutes (5-minute presentation plus question time)

Students will be allocated a time to meet with a member of the teaching team. At the beginning of the assessment, each student will nominate one of the required course readings from Weeks 1–5 (inclusive). The teaching staff member will then pose a question based on the nominated reading. The question will take the following form: 'What is Text A about, and what insights does it offer for understanding one or more contemporary global crises?'

Responses should draw primarily on the nominated text. References to lecture materials are permitted but not required. No additional research is necessary. The assessment is designed to evaluate students' understanding of their chosen text and their ability to relate its key arguments and concepts to contemporary global issues.

Following the 5-minute presentation, students will be asked two follow-up questions. These questions are intended to provide an opportunity to clarify or expand on their responses and further demonstrate their understanding of the reading.

The task will be conducted during Week 6 (the non-teaching week allocated for oral presentations).

Presentations will be audio-recorded to ensure fairness and to support marking moderation and appeals processes, if required.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Students will be given a selection of contemporary global crisis scenarios and asked to produce a 2,000-word evidence-based policy report (not an essay). The purpose of this assessment is to develop students’ ability to critically evaluate evidence, compare competing perspectives, and use generative AI responsibly and effectively in policy analysis.

Due date: 6th of October, 2 pm.

Using AI

Students are required to make responsible and meaningful use of generative AI as part of their research and analytical process. There is no single prescribed method of AI use. Rather, students should make deliberate decisions about how and why AI is used to support their work. Students may adopt different approaches, including source-led analysis, AI-assisted research, or other appropriate methods.

The Task

Prepare a 2,000-word policy report (excluding the bibliography) for a decision-maker addressing one contemporary global crisis. The report should:

Identify and analyse competing interpretations: Drawing on academic, policy, and other relevant sources, outline the common ways the crisis and its causes are understood.

Evaluate underlying assumptions: Analyse the assumptions, perspectives, positionalities, and interests that shape competing interpretations of the crisis.

Define the crisis: Clearly and concisely explain how you understand and define the crisis, why this interpretation is the most convincing, and why alternative interpretations are less persuasive.

Develop policy recommendations: Present clear and actionable recommendations to address, mitigate, or respond to the crisis. Recommendations should follow logically from the analysis presented in the report.

Reflect on AI use: Explain how AI was used in the research and analytical process. Readers should be able to understand how AI was used, how information was verified, how potential biases were addressed, and how sources were selected and incorporated into the final report.

Complete an AI Use Acknowledgement Form: it must be consistent with UQ requirements.

Include any relevant visualisations, tables, or figures in the policy analysis (where applicable, optional)

Further guidance and a detailed marking criteria matrix will be provided via Blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignments for this course (AI-Assisted Policy Analysis) will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The final piece of assessment for this course will be a central exam conducted during the central exam period. Students will have 2 hours to complete the exam. 

Students will be required to answer 2 questions in essay form. Students will be required to complete one question from Part A of the exam and the other from Part B. The topics covered in Part A correspond approximately with the first half of the course, while the topics covered in Part B correspond with the lectures in the second half of the course. Students will choose 1 of 6 questions from Part A and 1 of 6 from Part B.

The exam will be scheduled during the Central Exam period

Each of the 2 questions will be worth 15 marks. The maximum score for the assessment will be 30 marks.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.
